---
title: "BEAT-CF-MARS"
date: "18 June 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    logo: ""
    transition: slide
    toc: false
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
embed-resources: true
---


## Overview

Participants enter and remain in MARS for a 2-year period over we observe zero, one or more exacerbations for each individual.

  1. On exacerbation being reported, randomise ABX strategy to: SOC · early discontinuation · deferred treatment 
  2. Start surveilance to determine day participant returns to *healthy*.
  3. Repeat.

Some implications:

  - Participants may enter under randomised treatment more than once
  - Treatments are randomised (may get the same or something different)
  - Attractive to consumers
  - Definition of exacerbation/healthy state that can be readily elicited
  - Statistical pros and cons (efficiency, complexity, assumptions)

## Design

Process maps to a multi-state transition model, essentially a survival perspective:

![](../fig/msm-01.drawio.png){width=500 height=100 fig-align="center"}

Presently MARS tries to address:

*Among CF patients who experience an exacerbation that requires treatment, what is the difference in duration of exacerbation symptom burden under alternative antibiotic strategies?*

Under this approach, data requirements are relatively straight-forward:

+ timing of whenever someone gets sick and 
+ timing of whenever someone gets well, over the two year period

plus some baseline data for prognostic factors (age etc)

## Analysis

+ The analysis presently constrains view to a fixed window and evaluates total burden (duration) of symptoms within that window.

+ Considered:
  - exacerbation days to 1-3 months modelling simple means 
  - sojourn times (as arising from MSM)
  - restricted mean survival times (only handles a single event within the window)

+ The length of the window dictates the exacerbation symptom burden we observe.

+ We are considering other options as well, e.g. joint survival for time to recovery, time to relapse.

## Decision Framework

An observation from previous discussions is the need to capture both the impacts on recovery and relapse. The question is how to integrate these into a single coherent decision framework.

+ Does treatment extend recovery relative to SOC?
+ Does treatment reduce the time to next exacerbation relative to SOC?

That is, does a given treatment carry over into the recovered state (or further)?

Total burden (duration) of symptoms within a window and comparing this across groups gives us a way to base the decision framework on a single metric rather than a joint or gatekeeper style decision framework.

## Scenarios

+ Example scenarios:

![](../fig/sim16-2-scenario.png){fig-align="center"}

+ With decision rules acting on the parameter estimates derived from the model.


## Model Parameters

+ Under a linear approximation, parameters of interest arise directly from the model

![](../fig/sim16-2-posterior.png){fig-align="center"}

+ Where the above shows the expectation and distribution of the posterior means over a set of simulations

## Decision Probability

+ And the probability of decisions under this setup are reasonable

![](../fig/sim16-2-decision-prob.png){fig-align="center"}

+ But are less acceptable when smaller fixed time horizons are considered motivating further exploration of options.

## Thanks

Questions, comments?
